title: Hebron (entry, vol. 2, pp. 606-609)
author: Avraham (Avi) Ofer
publisher: The New Encyclopedia of Archaeological Excavations in the Holy Land, ed. Ephraim Stern (Israel Exploration Society / Carta, Jerusalem)
allowedUseNotes: Copyrighted reference-encyclopedia entry. Project stores its own summary of the excavation findings only; no reproduction of plans, sections, or photographs.
summary: Avi Ofer directed the Judean Hills Survey Expedition's 1984-1986 excavation season at Tel Rumeida (identified with biblical Hebron), building on the 1964-1966 American expedition led by P.C. Hammond. Ofer's NEAEHL entry is the standard published account of the site's excavation results across periods. Relevant to early Iron IIA town form (the gap flagged by claim-hebron-town-form): occupation at the tell is continuous from the Middle Bronze Age into Iron Age II; the massive Middle Bronze 'cyclopean' fortification wall was not replaced by a newly built Iron Age wall but continued in reuse into Iron Age I and II, and Ofer reads the 11th-10th century BCE horizon (the Iron I/early Iron IIA transition, contemporary with the setting of 2 Samuel 2) as one of the site's population peaks. Distinct architectural remains specifically datable to Iron IIA proper (10th-9th century BCE) are comparatively sparse and scattered relative to the well-preserved reused Bronze Age fortification and the more substantial later Iron IIB/C (8th-6th century) building remains reported by more recent excavations (Eisenberg and Ben-Shlomo's Tel Hevron 2014 excavations).
confidenceNotes: TO VERIFY: this project has not directly inspected the NEAEHL volume itself. The summary above is assembled from cross-corroborating secondary/tertiary sources describing Ofer's survey and its conclusions (a Wikipedia 'Kingdom of Judah' synthesis citing the Iron IIA sparse-remains point; Emek Shaveh's public Tel Rumeida site guide; TheTorah.com's 'The Ancient City of Hebron' by David Ben-Shlomo, co-director of the more recent Tel Hevron excavations, describing continuous/reinforced fortifications through the First Temple period) rather than the original NEAEHL page-606-609 text. Exact page-level citation for each specific claim (the 'population peak' framing especially) is not independently confirmed. This is enough to move claim-hebron-town-form off a pure identification-only citation gap and onto a real, named excavation source describing Iron I/early Iron IIA-horizon activity at the site, but not enough to assert a specific excavated town plan, wall circuit, or footprint for the early 10th century BCE specifically — the sparse-remains caveat is the operative limit.
